expectations.text: Reads and references the scope doc — skips problem/motivation domain since it was resolved in scope
expectations.evidence: Transcript opening shows the assistant summarized the scope doc: 'I've read the scope for Digital Waivers. Problem: paper waivers cause slow check-in...' The interview then starts at Domain 2 (Target Users), skipping Domain 1 (Problem/Motivation). metrics.json confirms: domains_skipped: ['problem_and_motivation'], skip_reason: 'Scope doc already resolved problem statement and motivation'. The scope is referenced throughout -- e.g., 'The scope mentions customers, front-desk staff, and managers' (Domain 2), 'The scope includes minor/guardian signing' (Domain 3), 'The scope lists waiver analytics or reporting dashboards as out' (Domain 7), 'The scope identifies one [risk]: native e-signature may not meet legal requirements' (Domain 9).

expectations.text: No technical implementation details (schemas, APIs, architecture, build phases, technology selection)
expectations.evidence: The interview references codebase knowledge in product terms only: 'The app currently has three roles: owner, admin, and member' (not 'permissions.ts defines ac.newRole'), 'The customer model captures first name, last name, email...' (not column names), 'The platform already uses Resend for email' (not import paths). metrics.json confirms 0 technical leakage instances. The PRD itself contains no database schemas, API endpoints, component names, or architecture decisions. FR items specify what (e.g., 'FR-7: Public URL at {org-slug}.rangeops.com/waiver') not how (no Next.js route file paths, no middleware configs).

expectations.text: Covers all 9 PRD domains (problem skip OK if scope exists): target users, user stories, functional requirements, non-functional requirements, success metrics, scope, dependencies, risks
expectations.evidence: The transcript covers 8 interview domains (problem skipped per scope): Domain 2 Target Users (lines 17-59), Domain 3 User Stories (lines 63-108), Domain 4 Functional Requirements (lines 112-233), Domain 5 Non-Functional Requirements (lines 237-279), Domain 6 Success Metrics (lines 283-314), Domain 7 Scope Definition (lines 318-362), Domain 8 Dependencies & Constraints (lines 366-397), Domain 9 Risks & Open Questions (lines 400-445). The PRD output has all sections including Problem Statement (written from scope), Target Users, User Stories, Functional Requirements, Non-Functional Requirements, Success Metrics, Scope (in/out/future), Dependencies & Constraints, Risks & Open Questions.

expectations.text: User stories follow As-a/I-want/So-that format with acceptance criteria (checkboxes)
expectations.evidence: All 7 user stories in prd.md use the format. Example: 'As a range manager, I want to create a waiver template with formatted legal text and an expiration period, so that customers sign a professional document and I know when re-signing is needed.' Each story has acceptance criteria with checkbox format, e.g., '- [ ] Manager navigates to Settings > Waivers'. Stories: Create Waiver Template (7 criteria), Edit and Republish Template (4), Sign Waiver Adult (10), Sign Waiver Guardian for Minor (7), Check Waiver Status at Check-In (4), View Waiver Coverage (3), Auto-Link and Create Customers (4).

expectations.text: Quality gate automatically runs after PRD — delivers Ready/Revise/Rethink verdict with strengths and issues
expectations.evidence: quality-gate.md exists with a clear verdict: '### Verdict: Ready'. It includes a Strengths section with 4 bullet points (persona coverage, codebase alignment, scope control, success metrics) and an Issues section with 2 items (routing concern flagged as minor, NFR-6 flagged as very minor). Also includes a Risks section flagging template versioning for downstream awareness. metrics.json confirms: quality_gate.ran: true, verdict: 'Ready', issues_count: 2, issues_severity: ['minor', 'very_minor'].

expectations.text: Glossary conditions checked after quality gate — generates glossary if 3+ domain nouns or naming conflicts detected
expectations.evidence: glossary.md was produced. metrics.json documents the trigger conditions: 'PRD introduces 10+ domain nouns not in codebase (waiver template, template version, signed waiver, waiver status, expiration period, e-signature, audit metadata, guardian, minor, waiver coverage, signing page)', plus two naming conflicts ('waiver' refers to both template and signed record; 'member' overloaded between staff role and customer membership), plus a bounded context crossing. The glossary defines 17 terms and resolves 3 ambiguities. The conditions clearly exceed the 3+ domain nouns threshold.

expectations.text: Suggests /design (UI features) or /architect (backend-only) as next step
expectations.evidence: metrics.json includes next_step.suggested: '/design' with reason: 'Feature has significant UI surface area: public signing page (first public-facing page), template editor with rich text, waiver status badges in existing customer table, guardian/minor flow. UI design decisions needed before architecture.' This is the correct recommendation since digital waivers is heavily UI-facing.

expectations.text: PRD has key sections: Problem Statement, Target Users (with personas), User Stories, Functional Requirements (FR-N numbered), Non-Functional Requirements, Success Metrics table, Scope (in/out/future), Dependencies & Constraints, Risks & Open Questions, Pipeline Status
expectations.evidence: prd.md contains all listed sections: Problem Statement (line 6), Target Users with 4 personas (Range Manager, Front-Desk Staff, Range Customer, Guardian -- each with Who/Current behavior/Key need/Usage frequency at lines 10-34), User Stories (7 stories with As-a format, lines 36-104), Functional Requirements (FR-1 through FR-31, numbered, lines 105-146), Non-Functional Requirements (NFR-1 through NFR-6, lines 148-156), Success Metrics table with 5 rows including Metric/Target/Measurement/Timeframe columns (lines 158-165), Scope with In scope/Out of scope/Future sections (lines 167-199), Dependencies & Constraints (lines 201-208), Risks & Open Questions (lines 210-216), Pipeline Status table (lines 218-223).

claims.evidence: Counted 27 AskUserQuestion-style interactions in the transcript (each with Header/Options blocks), spanning domains 2-9. Domains match the listed 8: target_users_and_personas, user_stories_and_jtbd, functional_requirements, non_functional_requirements, success_metrics_and_kpis, scope_definition, dependencies_and_constraints, risks_and_open_questions.

Reviewed all 27 questions and assistant responses in the transcript. Technical knowledge is surfaced as product behavior: 'The app currently has three roles' (not code references), 'The customer model captures...' (not schema column names), 'The platform already uses Resend' (not import paths). The PRD output contains no database schemas, API routes, component names, or architecture prescriptions.

claims.claim: Quality gate verified against actual codebase
claims.evidence: quality-gate.md includes specific codebase verification: permission stubs in permissions.ts, customer schema fields, organization model fields (name, logo, branding JSON), subdomain routing via proxy middleware, Settings tabbed layout pattern, Resend email integration. These reference real codebase artifacts verified against actual code.

glossary.md has 3 entries under 'Ambiguities Resolved': (1) 'Waiver' refers to two things (template vs signed record), (2) 'Member' is overloaded (staff role vs customer membership), (3) 'Signature' vs 'e-signature' vs 'consent'. Each has Problem/Resolution/Action sections.
metrics.json states files_examined: 18, but the transcript does not show individual file reads -- it is a simulated interview transcript. Cannot independently verify the count.

suggestions.assertion: Quality gate automatically runs after PRD — delivers Ready/Revise/Rethink verdict with strengths and issues
suggestions.reason: This assertion would pass even if the quality gate were a rubber stamp that always says 'Ready.' Consider adding an assertion that the quality gate references specific codebase findings -- it's the codebase verification that makes this quality gate valuable vs. a generic checklist. In this case it did excellent codebase verification, but the assertion doesn't require it.

No assertion checks whether the PRD decisions actually match the user's answers from the interview. For example, the user said '80% adoption' and the PRD says '80%+' -- good. But if the PRD had silently changed a user decision (e.g., adding multiple templates when the user said one), no assertion would catch that. An assertion like 'PRD decisions are traceable to specific user responses in the transcript' would catch silent overrides.

No assertion checks the quality or substance of the interview questions themselves -- whether they were relevant, codebase-informed, and non-leading. The transcript shows strong questioning (e.g., 'The app currently has three roles' informed the permissions question), but a skill that asked 27 trivial yes/no questions would also pass the '27 questions across 8 domains' metric.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions cover structural completeness well but could be stronger on content fidelity -- verifying that PRD content actually traces back to user decisions, and that interview questions demonstrate real codebase awareness rather than just counting them.
